content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 15,
1995
Honorable Wyatt Hart
Councilmember
City of San Juan Capistrano
28451 Ortega Highway
San Juan Capistrano,
CA
92675
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-131
Dear Councilmember Hart:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard
ing your responsibilities under the conflict-of—interest provi
sions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Please note that nothing in this letter should b construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
Is your ownership of a mobile home jointly with Steve Nordeck
and his spouse in San Clemente an economic interest for purposes
of the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?
CONCLUS ION
Based on your facts,
you do not have an economic interest in
the Nordecks or the mobile home that would result in disclosure or
disqualification obligations.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—95—l31
Page
2
FACTS
2
In November 1994 you were elected to the San Juan Capistrano
City Council.
On March 1,
1994,
you and your spouse and Steve
Nordeck and his spouse jointly purchased a mobile home located in
the City of San Clemente.
The mobile home is located on leased
property in San Clemente.
You stated in our telephone
conversation of May 4,
1995, that the mobile home is more than two
miles from the boundaries of San Juan Capistrano.
According to your letter, the mobile home is used as a second
home and not as income producing property.
You stated that no
written agreement exists between you and the Nordecks with respect
to the mobile home, but that you and the Nordecks share costs and
expenses equally.
In our telephone conversation of May 1,
1995,
you stated that use of the mobile home was also equally divided.
You stated that Mr.
Nordeck also owns property in the City of
San Juan Capistrano and operates a restaurant business in the
city.
You stated that periodically Mr. Nordeck appears before the
San Juan Capistrano City Council for various approvals and other
matters.
You anticipate that he will be seeking to buy or lease a
restaurant currently owned by the city.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
A “public official” is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural
person who is a member, officer,
employee,
or consultant of a
state or local government agency.
This definition would include a
member of the San Juan Capistrano City Council.
Section 87103 provides:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
2
The following facts are based on your letter and our telephone
conversations of May 1,
1995 and May 4,
1995.


Our File No.
A—95—13l
Page
3
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
The amount of the value of
gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the Commission to equal the
same amount determined by the Commission pursuant
to subdivision
(d)
of Section 89504.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Your facts do not involve investment interests in, or
positions with any business entity that will be potentially
affected by the decisions in question.
(Section 87103(a)
and
(d).)
Section 82005 defines “business entity” as any organization
or enterprise operated for profit,
including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership,
firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association.
You stated that the mobile
home in question is used solely for personal purposes and is not
operated for profit.
Thus, your interest in the mobile home is
not an interest in a business entity under the Act.
However,
you do have an ownership interest in the property in
question.
Generally, ownership interests in real property are
disciosable economic interests.
However,
Section 82033 defines an
“interest in real property” to include any leasehold, beneficial
or ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in


Our File No.
A-95—131
Page 4
real property located in the lurisdiction.
Real property is
located in the jurisdiction only if it is in the City of San Juan
Capistrano,
or it is located within (or not more than)
two miles
of the boundaries of the city or any land owned or used by the
city.
In our telephone conversation of May 4,
1995,
you indicated
that the property on which the mobile home was situated was more
than two miles from the boundaries of the City of San Juan
Capistrano.
Thus, the property would not be an economic interest
of yours under the Act.
Finally, the Nordecks may constitute an economic interest
under the Act if they are a source of $250 or more in income to
you in the past 12 months.
You stated that you paid one-half of
the purchase price and one—half of all the expenses related to the
mobile home and that your use of the mobile home is no more than
one-half the time.
In the Lee Advice Letter,
No. A-81-12-504,
we
advised:
The mere sharing of ownership does not cause
one owner to become a source of income to the
other.
Absent specific facts which indicate that
[the co—owner] is a source of income to [the
official],
such as some other financial
relationship existing between them, which is not
disclosed by the facts. .., there is no conflict of
interest under the Political Reform Act.
Thus,
based on your facts,
and assuming no other financial
relationship exists between you and Mr. Nordeck,
3 Mr.
Nordeck will
not be considered a source of income to you.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59O1.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
We also have not considered other possible basis of economic
interests,
such as receipt of gifts from Mr.
Nordeck.
Our
analysis is limited to the joint ownership of the mobile home.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.

QUESTION: Is your ownership of a mobile home jointly with Steve Nordeck
and his spouse in San Clemente an economic interest for purposes
of the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION: Based on your facts,
you do not have an economic interest in
the Nordecks or the mobile home that would result in disclosure or
disqualification obligations.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No.
A—95—l31
